briefly_syntheses.sha256:57a15f04f8794485f464257a829a404cd: Here's a 30-60 second spoken summary in Jamie's voice: --- There's a mix of the playful and the serious this week. The LEGO-Crocs partnership dropped on April first, which feels about right. On the more earnest side, a piece about whether our blogs will outlive us landed close to home — the answer, at least for Jamie, is to keep things simple: text, files, cheap hosting. Om Malik shows up twice, once on why tech and media have such a complicated relationship, and again on what he's calling the announcement economy — the idea that announcements are just memes now, which tracks with what Scott Galloway has said about storytelling being the most valuable skill around. OpenAI shipped a Codex app, which is an improvement, though the observation is that they still lag in the programming space because their whole approach is just different. Ethereum Name Service keeps building what feels like genuine utility in a space full of noise, and GitHub is being smart by letting teams bring whatever agents they want. And there's a map-to-poster tool that's visually satisfying, even if the coordinate control could use some work. All of these are linked in the web archive.

briefly_syntheses.sha256:4b1e407713d588b8ec557672285068723: There's a lot of ground covered this week. On the technology side, ENS is staying on Ethereum's Layer 1, which feels like good news for one of the more meaningful applications in that space. Agentic commerce is getting serious attention — both O'Reilly and Snowflake are mapping out how AI agents will reshape shopping, marketing, and payments. And there's a personal note: SPS Commerce just introduced MAX, a product Jamie was part of building, and the excitement there is genuine. On the surveillance front, a piece on Ring cameras lands with some editorial weight — the argument being that consumer-grade doorbell cameras are quietly assembling a dragnet that anyone can access for a price. The UniFi alternative gets a mention. Then there's music, which shows up twice. Andrea Bocelli's Nessun dorma from the Winter Olympics opening ceremony apparently nearly brought tears — Pavarotti's version is the lasagna-making standard, but this one cleared the bar. And a local Minnesota musician, Humbird, gets a warm nod. Gaming culture gets a moment too, with Lil Wayne crossing over into Clash Royale territory. All of these are linked in the web archive.

briefly_syntheses.sha256:cd601d345f007f136414f62e9bb4a0c9a: The open web keeps coming up, and Doc Searls puts it plainly — the internet can't just be malls. We need something more like public parks. That framing stuck with me. And on the agentic side, there's a nice observation that the old Unix command line virtues — non-blocking, good flags, solid documentation — turn out to be exactly what AI agents need. Which is part of why I built mb, my agent-first micro.blog client, the way I did. Old ideas, new reasons to care about them. All of these are linked in the web archive.

briefly_syntheses.sha256:46a0f486c9039fcfb4b8a2b6731e139e5: Here's a 30-60 second spoken summary in Jamie's voice: --- There's a lot of agentic AI energy in this batch. Remote sessions with Claude Code, scheduled prompt loops, Stripe making token-based billing trivially easy — the infrastructure for agents doing real work is quietly snapping into place. On a lighter note, there's a video of ringing the Nasdaq closing bell, which is a moment worth marking. The phishing story from Matt Mullenweg is a good reminder that even people who know better can get close to the edge — worth reading carefully. RSS keeps coming up as a theme, and there's a nice blog discovery tool to go along with it. Claude for Excel apparently embarrasses Microsoft's own Copilot effort, which feels like a fair observation. ENS gets some attention too, with a longer view that crypto infrastructure becomes more interesting once agents need to actually move money around. And somewhere in there, a tool that turns your handwriting into a font, which has been on the wishlist for a while. All of these are linked in the web archive.

briefly_syntheses.sha256:d86f5825994f0904b68ed0f9ed3133eae: Here's a 30-60 second spoken summary in Jamie's voice: --- This week's Briefly section wanders across a few different obsessions. There's a thread running through several links about agentic AI — what it looks like when systems get more capable, how people are actually building with them, and some honest skepticism along the way. The Obsidian note on agents using it as a knowledge store is a good example: maybe that tool was never meant for humans in the first place. Bitcoin's miner reward math gets a look, with some real uncertainty about whether transaction fees can carry the ecosystem long-term. On the hardware side, a new Bambu Lab 3D printer and a comparison to how DJI took over drones — that framing landed as reassuring, not just hype. There's a security reminder that WordPress plugins remain a supply chain waiting to go wrong. And then some genuine delight: NASA Artemis wallpapers, a trip into Nintendo DS programming, and the Apple Frames shortcut doing things that make you appreciate what Shortcuts can actually do. Neurosymbolic AI gets a flag as something worth paying attention to, and Ethereum's slow, quiet progress gets a mention while everyone else is looking the other direction. All of these are linked in the web archive.

briefly_syntheses.sha256:38f8c9fd86c43b17e7b5b5df0ce9a9bd0: This week's Briefly section wanders across a few different eras of technology and culture. There's a deep-dive into the Ada programming language — the argument being that it solved problems decades before the rest of the industry caught up. On the current end of things, a look at how Claude's system prompt shifted between two recent releases, which is a small window into how these models actually change under the hood. A good chunk of the links orbit Apple — Tim Cook's community letter, John Gruber's take on the CEO transition, and the less comfortable territory of Trump weighing in. Respect for what Cook built, some unease about recent directions, and not much to add on that last one. There's a personal thread running through it too. A Noah Kahan Tiny Desk concert — the whole family is into his music and they've got tickets this summer in Minneapolis. And a genuinely nostalgic detour into Zip drives, that brief moment in the nineties when a hundred megabytes felt like infinite space. Rounding it out, a piece on using wikis as a collaboration layer with AI agents, and an Acquired episode on Ferrari that's worth the time if you follow Formula 1 at all. All of these are linked in the web archive.
briefly_syntheses.sha256:197b948133ffed3a5377f0837b8767ff1: The Briefly section this week wanders across a few different worlds. There's a deep dive into the Ada programming language — the argument being that it solved problems decades before the rest of the industry caught up. A piece on using wikis as a collaboration layer with AI agents, which feels like a practical answer to a real workflow problem. And some genuinely interesting detail on what changed between Claude releases at the system prompt level. A good chunk of the links circle around Apple and Tim Cook — Gruber weighs in a couple of times on the CEO transition, and there's a Trump moment that gets a grimacing emoji and nothing more, which feels about right. On a warmer note, the whole family has tickets to see Noah Kahan in Minneapolis this summer, and the Tiny Desk concert is worth your time. And there's a lovely nostalgia hit about Zip drives — that mix of excitement and obsolescence that defined so much of nineties computing. Rounding it out, a long-form Acquired episode on Ferrari, which lands differently when you're actually a fan of the team. All of these are linked in the web archive.
